text: evanverse 目前正在积极维护中。CRAN 版本已被归档，v0.5.2 修复完成后将重新提交。 开发版可通过 GitHub 安装：

title: evanverse
text: evanverse 是一个 R 工具包，提供约 50 个函数，涵盖包管理、数据可视化、统计检验、生物信息学等领域。由 Evan Zhou 开发。
code: library(evanverse)  "你好" %p% "世界"                      # 字符串拼接 inst_pkg("limma", source = "Bioc")    # 多源安装包 plot_venn(list(A = 1:5, B = 3:8))    # 快速绘制韦恩图 quick_ttest(df, "group", "value")     # 自动选择 t 检验方法 gene2ensembl(c("TP53", "BRCA1"))      # 基因 ID 转换

title: evanverse
text: 系统要求： R ≥ 4.1.0
code: # 稳定版（CRAN） install.packages("evanverse")  # 开发版 devtools::install_github("evanbio/evanverse")

title: evanverse
text: check_pkg() — 检查包是否已安装 inst_pkg() — 从 CRAN / GitHub / Bioconductor 安装 update_pkg() — 更新已安装的包 pkg_version() — 获取包版本 pkg_functions() — 列出包中的函数 set_mirror() — 配置 CRAN 镜像 plot_venn() — 韦恩图 plot_forest() — 森林图 plot_bar() — 柱状图 plot_pie() — 饼图 plot_density() — 密度图 quick_ttest() — 自动方法选择的 t 检验 quick_anova() — 单因素方差分析（含事后检验） quick_chisq() — 卡方 / Fisher 精确检验 quick_cor() — 相关性分析（含热力图） stat_power() — 统计功效分析 stat_n() — 样本量计算 get_palette() — 获取配色 list_palettes() — 列出可用配色 create_palette() — 创建自定义配色 preview_palette() — 预览配色 palette_gallery() — 浏览全部配色 compile_palettes() — 编译配色数据 remove_palette() — 删除配色 hex2rgb() / rgb2hex() — 颜色格式转换 download_url() — 下载单个 URL download_batch() — 批量下载 download_geo() — 下载 GEO 数据集 file_info() — 文件元信息 file_ls() — 列出文件及元信息 file_tree() — 目录树可视化 view() — 交互式数据查看 gene2ensembl() — 基因符号转 Ensembl ID gene2entrez() — 基因符号转 Entrez ID download_gene_ref() — 下载基因参考表 gmt2df() — GMT 解析为数据框 gmt2list() — GMT 解析为列表 df2list() — 数据框转命名列表 df2vect() — 数据框列转命名向量 recode_column() — 按映射表重编码列值 %p% — 字符串拼接 %% — 身份比较 %nin% — 非成员判断 %map% — 映射操作符 %match% — 匹配操作符 comb() — 组合数 perm() — 排列数 toy_gene_ref() — 用于测试的小型基因参考表 toy_gmt() — 用于测试的小型 GMT 文件

title: Base Utilities
text: base module provides eleven utility functions covering four areas:
code: library(evanverse)

what: df2list() — Split a data frame into a named list
title: Base Utilities
text: Groups one column’s values another column returns named list. Useful building marker lists, gene set inputs, grouping operation downstream functions expect list.
code: df <- data.frame(   cell_type = c("T_cell", "T_cell", "B_cell", "B_cell", "B_cell"),   marker    = c("CD3D", "CD3E", "CD79A", "MS4A1", "CD19"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  df2list(df, group_col = "cell_type", value_col = "marker") #> $T_cell #> [1] "CD3D" "CD3E" #> #> $B_cell #> [1] "CD79A" "MS4A1" "CD19"

what: df2vect() — Extract a named vector from a data frame
title: Base Utilities
text: Extracts two columns returns named vector, using one column names values. original value type preserved. name column must contain NA, empty strings, duplicates — three caught input raise informative error.
code: df <- data.frame(   gene  = c("TP53", "BRCA1", "MYC"),   score = c(0.91, 0.74, 0.55),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  df2vect(df, name_col = "gene", value_col = "score") #>  TP53 BRCA1   MYC #>  0.91  0.74  0.55 bad <- data.frame(id = c("a", "a"), val = 1:2) df2vect(bad, "id", "val") #> Error in `df2vect()`: #> ! `name_col` contains duplicate values.

what: recode_column() — Map column values via a named vector
title: Base Utilities
text: Replaces values column using named vector (dict). Unmatched values receive default (NA default). Set name write new column instead overwriting source.
code: df <- data.frame(   gene = c("TP53", "BRCA1", "EGFR", "XYZ"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  dict <- c("TP53" = "Tumour suppressor", "EGFR" = "Oncogene")  # Overwrite in place recode_column(df, column = "gene", dict = dict) #>                gene #> 1 Tumour suppressor #> 2              <NA> #> 3          Oncogene #> 4              <NA>  # Write to a new column, keep original; use a custom fallback recode_column(df, column = "gene", dict = dict,               name = "role", default = "Unknown") #>    gene              role #> 1  TP53 Tumour suppressor #> 2 BRCA1           Unknown #> 3  EGFR          Oncogene #> 4   XYZ           Unknown

title: Base Utilities
text: Returns interactive reactable widget search, filtering, sorting, pagination. RStudio widget renders Viewer pane; environments renders default HTML output. view() requires reactable package. installed, function raises clear error rather falling back silently.
code: view(iris, n = 10)

title: Base Utilities
text: Returns data frame file metadata files directory. Columns: file, size_MB, modified_time, path.
code: # All files in the current directory file_ls(".") #>              file size_MB       modified_time                          path #> 1  DESCRIPTION   0.002  2026-03-20 14:22:01  F:/project/evanverse/DESCRIPTION #> 2    NAMESPACE   0.002  2026-03-20 14:22:01  F:/project/evanverse/NAMESPACE #> ...  # R source files only, searched recursively file_ls("R", recursive = TRUE, pattern = "\\.R$")

title: Base Utilities
text: Returns four-column data frame file_ls() explicit vector file paths rather directory scan. Duplicate paths input silently deduplicated. Missing files raise error listing unresolved paths.
code: file_info(c("DESCRIPTION", "NAMESPACE")) #>          file size_MB       modified_time                          path #> 1 DESCRIPTION   0.002  2026-03-20 14:22:01  F:/project/evanverse/DESCRIPTION #> 2   NAMESPACE   0.002  2026-03-20 14:22:01  F:/project/evanverse/NAMESPACE

title: Base Utilities
text: Prints directory structure tree format. Returns lines invisibly output can captured needed.
code: file_tree(".", max_depth = 2) #> F:/project/evanverse #> +-- DESCRIPTION #> +-- NAMESPACE #> +-- R #> |   +-- base.R #> |   +-- plot.R #> |   +-- utils.R #> +-- tests #>     +-- testthat

title: Base Utilities
text: gene2entrez() gene2ensembl() accept character vector gene symbols return three-column data frame: original input (symbol), case-normalised form used matching (symbol_std), converted ID.

title: Base Utilities
text: Matching performed ref data frame columns symbol, entrez_id, ensembl_id. Two sources available:
code: # Fast, offline reference for development ref <- toy_gene_ref(species = "human")  # Full reference for analysis (requires network + Bioconductor) # ref <- download_gene_ref(species = "human")

title: Base Utilities
text: Unmatched symbols returned NA ID column rather dropped.

title: Base Utilities
code: ref <- toy_gene_ref(species = "human")  gene2entrez(c("tp53", "BRCA1", "GHOST"), ref = ref, species = "human") #>   symbol symbol_std entrez_id #> 1   tp53       TP53      7157 #> 2  BRCA1      BRCA1       672 #> 3  GHOST      GHOST      <NA>

title: Base Utilities
code: ref_mouse <- toy_gene_ref(species = "mouse")  gene2ensembl(c("Trp53", "TRP53", "FakeGene"), ref = ref_mouse, species = "mouse") #>     symbol symbol_std          ensembl_id #> 1    Trp53      trp53  ENSMUSG00000059552 #> 2    TRP53      trp53  ENSMUSG00000059552 #> 3 FakeGene   fakegene                <NA>

title: Base Utilities
text: GMT (Gene Matrix Transposed) standard format gene set collections MSigDB. line encodes one gene set: term, description, tab-separated list gene symbols. toy_gmt() writes minimal GMT file temp path offline use:
code: tmp <- toy_gmt(n = 3) readLines(tmp) #> [1] "HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY\tGenes regulated by p53\tTP53\tBRCA1\tMYC\t..." #> [2] "HALLMARK_MTORC1_SIGNALING\tGenes upregulated by mTORC1\tPTEN\t..." #> [3] "HALLMARK_HYPOXIA\tGenes upregulated under hypoxia\tMTOR\tHIF1A\t..."

title: Base Utilities
text: Returns one row per gene, making output directly compatible dplyr data.table workflows.
code: df <- gmt2df(tmp) head(df, 4) #>                      term               description  gene #> 1   HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY  Genes regulated by p53   TP53 #> 2   HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY  Genes regulated by p53  BRCA1 #> 3   HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY  Genes regulated by p53    MYC #> 4   HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY  Genes regulated by p53   EGFR

what: gmt2list() — Named list of gene vectors
title: Base Utilities
text: Returns named list element character vector gene symbols. format expected gene set enrichment tools (e.g., fgsea, clusterProfiler). Lines fewer 3 tab-separated fields skipped warning removed result. every line malformed, functions return NULL rather raising error — current behaviour. Always check NULL return parsing files untrusted sources.
code: gs <- gmt2list(tmp) names(gs) #> [1] "HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY"      "HALLMARK_MTORC1_SIGNALING" #> [3] "HALLMARK_HYPOXIA"  gs[["HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY"]] #>  [1] "TP53"   "BRCA1"  "MYC"    "EGFR"   "PTEN"   "CDK2"   "MDM2" #>  [8] "RB1"    "CDKN2A" "AKT1"

title: Base Utilities
text: Gene ID conversion GMT parsing compose naturally. example reads GMT file, converts gene symbols Entrez IDs, produces named list ID vectors ready enrichment analysis.
code: library(evanverse)  # 1. Parse GMT into long format tmp <- toy_gmt(n = 5) df  <- gmt2df(tmp)  # 2. Convert symbols to Entrez IDs ref    <- toy_gene_ref(species = "human") id_map <- gene2entrez(df$gene, ref = ref, species = "human")  # 3. Attach IDs and drop unmatched df$entrez_id <- id_map$entrez_id df <- df[!is.na(df$entrez_id), ]  # 4. Rebuild named list with Entrez IDs gs_entrez <- df2list(df, group_col = "term", value_col = "entrez_id") gs_entrez[["HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY"]] #> [1] "7157" "672"  "4609" "1956" "5728" "1031" "4193" "5925" "1029"  "207"

title: Get Started
text: evanverse utility toolkit combines package management, data helpers, plotting tools, statistical workflows, convenient operators. Note: code examples static (eval = FALSE). copy-paste everything fan starts roaring, congratulations: laptop entered “research mode”.
code: library(evanverse)

title: Get Started
text: Try operators first:
code: "Good" %p% "morning" #> [1] "Good morning"  c("A", "B", "C") %nin% c("B", "D") #> [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE  c("tp53", "egfr") %match% c("TP53", "MYC", "EGFR") #> [1] 1 3

title: Get Started
text: Use built-package-management helpers: Update packages needed:
code: set_mirror("all", "tuna")  inst_pkg("dplyr", source = "CRAN")  check_pkg(c("dplyr", "ggplot2"), source = "CRAN") #> # A tibble with installed status update_pkg(source = "CRAN")

title: Get Started
code: df <- data.frame(   group = c("A", "A", "B"),   gene  = c("TP53", "EGFR", "BRCA1"),   score = c(1.2, 0.8, 1.6) )  df2list(df, group_col = "group", value_col = "gene") #> $A #> [1] "TP53" "EGFR" #> $B #> [1] "BRCA1"  df2vect(df, name_col = "gene", value_col = "score") #>  TP53  EGFR BRCA1 #>   1.2   0.8   1.6

title: Get Started
code: path <- toy_gmt(n = 3)  gmt_df <- gmt2df(path) head(gmt_df, 3) #>                    term               description  gene #> 1 HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY   Genes regulated by p53  TP53 #> ...  gmt_list <- gmt2list(path) names(gmt_list) #> [1] "HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY" "HALLMARK_MTORC1_SIGNALING" "HALLMARK_HYPOXIA"

title: Get Started
text: Correlation summary:
code: set.seed(42) df <- data.frame(   group = rep(c("A", "B"), each = 30),   value = c(rnorm(30, 5), rnorm(30, 6)) )  res_t <- quick_ttest(df, group_col = "group", value_col = "value") print(res_t) #> t.test | p = ... res_cor <- quick_cor(mtcars) print(res_cor) #> pearson | ... vars | ... significant pairs

title: Get Started
code: plot_bar(   data = data.frame(category = c("A", "B", "C"), value = c(10, 7, 12)),   x_col = "category",   y_col = "value" ) #> # A ggplot object

title: Get Started
code: # 1) Prepare toy reference and gene sets ref  <- toy_gene_ref("human", n = 100) path <- toy_gmt(n = 3)  # 2) Parse GMT and convert gene IDs long <- gmt2df(path) idm  <- gene2entrez(long$gene, ref = ref, species = "human") long$entrez_id <- idm$entrez_id  # 3) Build list per term long2 <- long[!is.na(long$entrez_id), ] sets  <- df2list(long2, group_col = "term", value_col = "entrez_id")  # 4) Quick statistical and plotting step res <- quick_cor(mtcars[, c("mpg", "hp", "wt", "qsec")]) plot(res, type = "upper", show_sig = TRUE)

title: Installation Guide
text: set_mirror() controls mirror sources used inst_pkg() update_pkg(). Supported mirror names: CRAN-mirrors used repo = "":
code: # Set both CRAN + Bioconductor mirrors (default mirror: tuna) set_mirror() #> v CRAN mirror set to: https://mirrors.tuna.tsinghua.edu.cn/CRAN #> v Bioconductor mirror set to: https://mirrors.tuna.tsinghua.edu.cn/bioconductor  # CRAN only set_mirror("cran", "westlake")  # Bioconductor only set_mirror("bioc", "official") set_mirror("all", "aliyun") #> Error in `set_mirror()`: #> ! Mirror "aliyun" is CRAN-only and cannot be used with repo = "all".

what: inst_pkg() - Install from CRAN, GitHub, Bioconductor, or local file
title: Installation Guide
text: Local installation requires path: Already-installed packages skipped automatically.
code: inst_pkg("dplyr", source = "CRAN") inst_pkg("hadley/emo", source = "GitHub") inst_pkg("DESeq2", source = "Bioconductor") inst_pkg(source = "Local", path = "mypackage_1.0.0.tar.gz") inst_pkg(source = "Local") #> Error in `inst_pkg()`: #> ! Must provide `path` for local installation.

what: check_pkg() - Check and optionally auto-install missing packages
title: Installation Guide
text: GitHub source, use "user/repo" format:
code: check_pkg("ggplot2", source = "CRAN") #> # A tibble: 1 x 4 #>   package name    installed source #>   <chr>   <chr>   <lgl>     <chr> #> 1 ggplot2 ggplot2 TRUE      CRAN check_pkg(c("ggplot2", "fakepkg123"), source = "CRAN") #> v Installed: ggplot2 #> ! Missing: fakepkg123 check_pkg(c("ggplot2", "fakepkg123"), source = "CRAN", auto_install = TRUE) #> i Installing missing packages automatically... check_pkg("r-lib/devtools", source = "GitHub")

what: update_pkg() - Update all packages or specific targets
title: Installation Guide
text: pkg provided, source must also provided (stay "").
code: # Update all CRAN + Bioconductor packages update_pkg() # CRAN only update_pkg(source = "CRAN") # Specific package(s) update_pkg("ggplot2", source = "CRAN") update_pkg("hadley/ggplot2", source = "GitHub") update_pkg("DESeq2", source = "Bioconductor")

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: ops module provides five infix operators cover four common tasks: Note: code examples vignette static (eval = FALSE). Output hand-written reflect current implementation. modify operators, re-verify examples manually switch chunks eval = TRUE. operators accept character input validate arguments raise informative error non-character, NA, empty inputs. %nin% %% unrestricted — mirror base R behaviour type.
code: library(evanverse)

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: Concatenates two character vectors element-wise single space. Equivalent paste(lhs, rhs, sep = " ") reads naturally pipelines. length-1 operand recycled longer vector usual R fashion: Empty strings valid — space always inserted: NA values non-character inputs rejected:
code: "Hello" %p% "world" #> [1] "Hello world"  c("good", "hello") %p% c("morning", "world") #> [1] "good morning" "hello world" "Gene:" %p% c("TP53", "BRCA1", "MYC") #> [1] "Gene: TP53"  "Gene: BRCA1" "Gene: MYC" "" %p% "world" #> [1] " world" "Hello" %p% NA #> Error in `%p%()`: #> ! `rhs` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  123 %p% "world" #> Error in `%p%()`: #> ! `lhs` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: Returns TRUE every element x present table. concise alternative !(x %% table). %nin% mirrors %% exactly — accepts type follows base R semantics NA type coercion: R coerces types comparing, character strings can match numeric values printed representation: Empty vectors return zero-length results without error:
code: c("A", "B", "C") %nin% c("B", "D") #> [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE  1:5 %nin% c(2, 4) #> [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE # NA matches NA in the table NA %nin% c(NA, 1) #> [1] FALSE  # NA does not match non-NA elements NA %nin% c(1, 2) #> [1] TRUE c("1", "2") %nin% c(1, 2) #> [1] FALSE FALSE c("a", "b") %nin% character(0)   # nothing to be in #> [1] TRUE TRUE  character(0) %nin% c("a", "b") #> logical(0)

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: %match% %map% lower-case sides comparing, "tp53" "TP53" treated string. require non-NA, non-empty character vectors sides.

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: Like base::match(), case-insensitive. Returns integer vector positions; unmatched elements become NA. table contains duplicates index first match returned, matching base R behaviour: Duplicate elements x matched independently: Non-character inputs, NA values, empty vectors rejected sides:
code: c("tp53", "BRCA1", "egfr") %match% c("TP53", "EGFR", "MYC") #> [1]  1 NA  2 c("x") %match% c("X", "x", "X") #> [1] 1 c("tp53", "tp53") %match% c("TP53", "EGFR") #> [1] 1 1 # empty x character(0) %match% c("TP53") #> Error in `%match%()`: #> ! `x` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  # NA in x c("tp53", NA) %match% c("TP53") #> Error in `%match%()`: #> ! `x` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  # empty table c("tp53") %match% character(0) #> Error in `%match%()`: #> ! `table` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  # NA in table c("tp53") %match% c("TP53", NA) #> Error in `%match%()`: #> ! `table` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: Like %match%, returns named character vector instead indices. Names canonical entries table; values original elements x. Unmatched entries silently dropped. Output order follows x. Output order follows x, table: Unmatched elements dropped rather returned NA: nothing matches, empty named character vector returned: Duplicate elements x match retained: error rules %match% apply sides:
code: c("tp53", "brca1", "egfr") %map% c("TP53", "EGFR", "MYC") #>   TP53   EGFR #> "tp53" "egfr" c("egfr", "tp53") %map% c("TP53", "EGFR") #>   EGFR   TP53 #> "egfr" "tp53" c("akt1", "tp53") %map% c("TP53", "EGFR") #>   TP53 #> "tp53" c("none1", "none2") %map% c("TP53", "EGFR") #> named character(0) c("tp53", "tp53") %map% c("TP53", "EGFR") #>   TP53   TP53 #> "tp53" "tp53" # empty x character(0) %map% c("TP53") #> Error in `%map%()`: #> ! `x` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  # NA in x c("tp53", NA) %map% c("TP53") #> Error in `%map%()`: #> ! `x` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  # empty table c("tp53") %map% character(0) #> Error in `%map%()`: #> ! `table` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.  # NA in table c("tp53") %map% c("TP53", NA) #> Error in `%map%()`: #> ! `table` must be a non-empty character vector without NA values.

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: Wraps base::identical(). Returns single TRUE FALSE tolerance type attribute differences. Unlike ==, %% distinguishes types, names, storage mode: NULL NA variants handled correctly: %% accepts type — input restriction.
code: 1:3 %is% 1:3 #> [1] TRUE  "hello" %is% "hello" #> [1] TRUE  list(a = 1) %is% list(a = 1) #> [1] TRUE 1:3 %is% c(1, 2, 3)          # integer vs double #> [1] FALSE  c(a = 1, b = 2) %is% c(b = 1, a = 2)   # same values, different names #> [1] FALSE NULL %is% NULL #> [1] TRUE  NA %is% NA #> [1] TRUE  NA %is% NA_real_   # logical NA vs double NA #> [1] FALSE

title: Custom Infix Operators
text: operators compose naturally bioinformatics pipelines. example filters gene table canonical symbols, maps aliases official form, labels gene’s match status.
code: library(evanverse)  canonical <- c("TP53", "BRCA1", "EGFR", "MYC", "PTEN") query     <- c("tp53", "brca1", "AKT1", "egfr", "unknown")  # 1. Which queries are not in the canonical set (case-insensitive)? missing_idx <- which(is.na(query %match% canonical)) query[missing_idx] #> [1] "AKT1"    "unknown"  # 2. Map matched queries to their canonical names query %map% canonical #>    TP53   BRCA1    EGFR #> "tp53" "brca1"  "egfr"  # 3. Build an annotation column using %p% anno <- "Gene:" %p% canonical anno #> [1] "Gene: TP53"  "Gene: BRCA1" "Gene: EGFR"  "Gene: MYC"   "Gene: PTEN"  # 4. Check that the canonical list hasn't changed canonical %is% c("TP53", "BRCA1", "EGFR", "MYC", "PTEN") #> [1] TRUE

code: library(evanverse)

what: get_palette() — Retrieve a palette by name
title: Color Palette Management
text: Returns character vector HEX color codes. type omitted, palette type auto-detected. Use n take first N colors larger palette. type wrong, error message tells palette actually lives: Requesting colors palette contains raises informative error rather silently recycling:
code: # Auto-detect type get_palette("qual_vivid") #> [1] "#E64B35" "#4DBBD5" "#00A087" "#3C5488" "#F39B7F" "#8491B4"  # Specify type explicitly get_palette("qual_vivid", type = "qualitative") #> [1] "#E64B35" "#4DBBD5" "#00A087" "#3C5488" "#F39B7F" "#8491B4"  # Take only the first 3 colors get_palette("seq_blues", type = "sequential", n = 3) #> [1] "#deebf7" "#9ecae1" "#3182bd" get_palette("qual_vivid", type = "sequential") #> Error in `get_palette()`: #> ! Palette "qual_vivid" not found under "sequential", but exists under "qualitative". #> Try: get_palette("qual_vivid", type = "qualitative") get_palette("qual_softtrio", type = "qualitative", n = 10) #> Error in `get_palette()`: #> ! Palette "qual_softtrio" only has 3 colors, but requested 10.

title: Color Palette Management
text: Returns data frame columns name, type, n_color, colors. Filter one types type argument. Results sorted type, n_color, name default. Set sort = FALSE keep original file order.
code: list_palettes() #>              name       type n_color                                   colors #> 1    div_contrast  diverging       5  #2166ac, #92c5de, #f7f7f7, ... #> 2    div_fireice   diverging       7  #4575b4, #91bfdb, #e0f3f8, ... #> ... #> 12   qual_vivid   qualitative       6  #E64B35, #4DBBD5, #00A087, ... #> ... # Single type list_palettes(type = "qualitative")  # Multiple types list_palettes(type = c("sequential", "diverging"))

what: palette_gallery() — Browse all palettes at once
title: Color Palette Management

Filter type control many palettes appear per page: Access individual pages returned list: Set verbose = FALSE suppress progress messages calling palette_gallery() programmatically.

title: Color Palette Management
text: Writes named palette JSON file color_dir/<type>/<name>.json. directory created automatically exist. default, saving existing name raises error. Pass overwrite = TRUE replace : function invisibly returns list path info result can captured needed.
code: temp_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "palettes")  create_palette("blues", "sequential", c("#deebf7", "#9ecae1", "#3182bd"),                color_dir = temp_dir) #> v Palette saved: /tmp/.../palettes/sequential/blues.json  create_palette("qual_vivid", "qualitative",                c("#E64B35", "#4DBBD5", "#00A087"),                color_dir = temp_dir) #> v Palette saved: /tmp/.../palettes/qualitative/qual_vivid.json create_palette("blues", "sequential", c("#c6dbef", "#6baed6", "#2171b5"),                color_dir = temp_dir, overwrite = TRUE) #> i Overwriting existing palette: "blues" #> v Palette saved: /tmp/.../palettes/sequential/blues.json # Without overwrite = TRUE create_palette("blues", "sequential", c("#deebf7", "#9ecae1", "#3182bd"),                color_dir = temp_dir) #> Error in `create_palette()`: #> ! Palette "blues" already exists. Use `overwrite = TRUE` to replace.

what: hex2rgb() — HEX to RGB data frame
title: Color Palette Management
text: Converts character vector HEX codes data frame columns hex, r, g, b. 6-digit (#RRGGBB) 8-digit (#RRGGBBAA) codes accepted; alpha channel silently ignored. # prefix required; codes fail pattern NA values reported invalid:
code: hex2rgb("#FF8000") #>       hex   r   g b #> 1 #FF8000 255 128 0  hex2rgb(c("#FF8000", "#00FF00", "#3182bdB2")) #>          hex   r   g   b #> 1    #FF8000 255 128   0 #> 2    #00FF00   0 255   0 #> 3 #3182bdB2  49 130 189 # Missing '#' prefix hex2rgb("FF8000") #> Error in `hex2rgb()`: #> ! `hex` contains invalid HEX codes: "FF8000".  # NA is treated as an invalid code hex2rgb(c("#FF8000", NA)) #> Error in `hex2rgb()`: #> ! `hex` contains invalid HEX codes: NA.

what: rgb2hex() — RGB to HEX color codes
title: Color Palette Management
text: symmetric counterpart hex2rgb(). Accepts either numeric vector length 3 data frame returned hex2rgb(). Non-integer values rounded conversion. Typical failure cases vector form: data frame form:
code: # Single color as a length-3 vector rgb2hex(c(255, 128, 0)) #> [1] "#FF8000" # Round-trip: HEX -> RGB -> HEX rgb2hex(hex2rgb(c("#FF8000", "#00FF00"))) #> [1] "#FF8000" "#00FF00" # Non-integer values are rounded rgb2hex(c(254.7, 128.2, 0.4)) #> [1] "#FF8000" # Wrong length rgb2hex(c(255, 128)) #> Error in `rgb2hex()`: #> ! `rgb` must be a numeric vector of length 3.  # Value out of range rgb2hex(c(255, 128, 300)) #> Error in `rgb2hex()`: #> ! `rgb` values must be in [0, 255]. # Missing column rgb2hex(data.frame(r = 255, g = 128)) #> Error in `rgb2hex()`: #> ! `rgb` must have columns {"r", "g", "b"}. Missing: {"b"}.  # Column value out of range rgb2hex(data.frame(r = 255, g = 128, b = 300)) #> Error in `rgb2hex()`: #> ! Column "b" in `rgb` must be numeric with values in [0, 255].

title: Color Palette Management
text: palette conversion functions compose naturally. example picks qualitative palette, converts colors RGB downstream processing, rounds-trips back HEX verify information lost.
code: library(evanverse)  # 1. Retrieve a qualitative palette colors <- get_palette("qual_vivid", type = "qualitative", n = 4) colors #> [1] "#E64B35" "#4DBBD5" "#00A087" "#3C5488"  # 2. Convert to RGB for numeric manipulation rgb_df <- hex2rgb(colors) rgb_df #>       hex   r   g   b #> 1 #E64B35 230  75  53 #> 2 #4DBBD5  77 187 213 #> 3 #00A087   0 160 135 #> 4 #3C5488  60  84 136  # 3. Lighten each color by blending 50% toward white (255) rgb_light <- rgb_df rgb_light$r <- (rgb_light$r + 255) / 2 rgb_light$g <- (rgb_light$g + 255) / 2 rgb_light$b <- (rgb_light$b + 255) / 2  # 4. Convert back to HEX and preview light_hex <- rgb2hex(rgb_light) light_hex #> [1] "#F2A59A" "#A6DDEA" "#80CFC7" "#9EAAC4"  # 5. Save the new derived palette for reuse temp_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "palettes") create_palette("qual_vivid_light", "qualitative", light_hex,                color_dir = temp_dir) #> v Palette saved: /tmp/.../palettes/qualitative/qual_vivid_light.json  # 6. Verify the new palette is retrievable "qual_vivid_light" %in% list_palettes(type = "qualitative")$name #> [1] TRUE  # 7. Clean up unlink(temp_dir, recursive = TRUE)

text: pkg module provides six functions covering four areas: Note: code examples vignette static (eval = FALSE). Output hand-written reflect current implementation. Network-dependent functions (inst_pkg(), update_pkg(), pkg_version()) require active internet connection runtime.
code: library(evanverse)

title: Package Management
text: Configures CRAN /Bioconductor download mirrors used subsequent installation update calls. previous settings returned invisibly, making easy restore . Available mirrors: CRAN-mirrors used repo = "": previous settings returned invisibly easy restoration:
code: # Set both mirrors to Tsinghua (default) set_mirror() #> v CRAN mirror set to: https://mirrors.tuna.tsinghua.edu.cn/CRAN #> v Bioconductor mirror set to: https://mirrors.tuna.tsinghua.edu.cn/bioconductor  # CRAN only set_mirror("cran", "ustc") #> v CRAN mirror set to: https://mirrors.ustc.edu.cn/CRAN  # Bioconductor only set_mirror("bioc", "nju") #> v Bioconductor mirror set to: https://mirrors.nju.edu.cn/bioconductor set_mirror("all", "aliyun") #> Error in `set_mirror()`: #> ! Mirror "aliyun" is CRAN-only and cannot be used with `repo = "all"`. #> i Use `set_mirror("cran", "aliyun")` instead. #> i Shared mirrors: "official", "tuna", "ustc", "westlake", "nju" prev <- set_mirror("cran", "tuna") # ... do work ... options(prev)  # restore original mirror

title: Package Management
text: Installs packages CRAN, GitHub, Bioconductor, local file. Packages already installed automatically skipped. path required local installs — omitting raises error immediately: Already-installed packages skipped rather reinstalled:
code: # CRAN inst_pkg("ggplot2", source = "CRAN") #> i Installing from CRAN: ggplot2 #> v Installation complete!  # Multiple packages at once inst_pkg(c("dplyr", "tidyr", "purrr"), source = "CRAN") # GitHub — basic "user/repo" format inst_pkg("hadley/emo", source = "GitHub")  # devtools extended formats are also accepted: # @ref pins a specific branch, tag, or SHA; #pr installs from a pull request inst_pkg("tidyverse/ggplot2@v3.4.0", source = "GitHub") inst_pkg("r-lib/cli#123",            source = "GitHub") # Bioconductor inst_pkg("DESeq2",    source = "Bioconductor") inst_pkg("scRNAseq",  source = "Bioconductor") # Local file inst_pkg(source = "Local", path = "mypackage_1.0.0.tar.gz") inst_pkg(source = "Local") #> Error in `inst_pkg()`: #> ! Must provide `path` for local installation. inst_pkg("ggplot2", source = "CRAN") #> i Package ggplot2 is already installed. Skipped. #> v All packages already installed.

title: Package Management
text: Returns tibble reporting whether package installed. Pass auto_install = TRUE install missing packages automatically; single failure interrupt remaining checks. GitHub packages package column holds full "user/repo" string name holds just package name: Auto-install missing packages one call:
code: check_pkg("ggplot2", source = "CRAN") #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   package name    installed source #>   <chr>   <chr>   <lgl>     <chr> #> 1 ggplot2 ggplot2 TRUE      CRAN check_pkg(c("ggplot2", "fakepkg123"), source = "CRAN") #> v Installed: ggplot2 #> ! Missing: fakepkg123 #> # A tibble: 2 × 4 #>   package    name       installed source #>   <chr>      <chr>      <lgl>     <chr> #> 1 ggplot2    ggplot2    TRUE      CRAN #> 2 fakepkg123 fakepkg123 FALSE     CRAN check_pkg("r-lib/cli", source = "GitHub") #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   package   name  installed source #>   <chr>     <chr> <lgl>     <chr> #> 1 r-lib/cli cli   TRUE      GitHub check_pkg(c("ggplot2", "fakepkg123"), source = "CRAN", auto_install = TRUE) #> v Installed: ggplot2 #> ! Missing: fakepkg123 #> i Installing from CRAN: fakepkg123 #> ...

title: Package Management
text: Supports three update modes: full update across sources, source-specific bulk update, targeted update named packages. Providing pkg source = "Bioconductor" updates package — trigger full Bioconductor upgrade. Combining pkg source = "" error:
code: # Update all CRAN + Bioconductor packages update_pkg() #> i Checking for BiocManager updates... #> i Upgrading Bioconductor packages to version 3.21... #> v Bioconductor upgraded to version 3.21. #> i Updating all CRAN packages... #> v Update complete! # CRAN only update_pkg(source = "CRAN")  # Bioconductor only (full Bioc upgrade) update_pkg(source = "Bioconductor") # Specific packages update_pkg("ggplot2",            source = "CRAN") update_pkg("hadley/ggplot2",     source = "GitHub") update_pkg("DESeq2",             source = "Bioconductor") update_pkg("ggplot2") #> Error in `update_pkg()`: #> ! Must specify `source` when providing `pkg`.

title: Package Management
text: Returns data frame columns package, version (installed), latest (newest available), source (CRAN, Bioconductor, GitHub). Source detection automatic. Bioconductor mirror unreachable, pkg_version() degrades gracefully: emits warning treats Bioc packages “Found”. Switch official mirror retry: set_mirror("bioc", "official"). Uninstalled unknown packages return NA version "Found" source: GitHub-installed packages report installed commit SHA remote reference: Duplicate package names input silently deduplicated:
code: pkg_version(c("ggplot2", "dplyr")) #>   package version latest source #> 1 ggplot2   3.4.4  3.5.0   CRAN #> 2   dplyr   1.1.4  1.1.4   CRAN pkg_version("nonexistentpkg") #>        package version latest      source #> 1 nonexistentpkg    <NA>   <NA> Not Found pkg_version("emo") #>   package version  latest                      source #> 1     emo   0.0.1 a1b2c3d GitHub (hadley/emo@master) pkg_version(c("ggplot2", "ggplot2")) #>   package version latest source #> 1 ggplot2   3.4.4  3.5.0   CRAN

title: Package Management
text: Returns alphabetically sorted character vector exported symbols installed package. Use key filter case-insensitive keyword. unmatched keyword returns empty vector rather error: Querying uninstalled package raises informative error:
code: pkg_functions("evanverse") #>  [1] "check_pkg"       "compile_palettes" "create_palette"  "df2list" #>  [5] "df2vect"         "file_info"        "file_ls"         "file_tree" #>  ... # Filter by keyword (case-insensitive) pkg_functions("evanverse", key = "palette") #> [1] "compile_palettes" "create_palette"   "get_palette" #> [4] "list_palettes"    "palette_gallery"   "preview_palette" #> [7] "remove_palette"  pkg_functions("stats", key = "test") #> [1] "ansari.test"     "bartlett.test"   "binom.test" #> [4] "chisq.test"      "cor.test"        "fisher.test" #> ... pkg_functions("stats", key = "zzzzzz") #> character(0) pkg_functions("somefakepkg") #> Error in `pkg_functions()`: #> ! Package `somefakepkg` is not installed.

title: Package Management
text: functions compose naturally setting new analysis environment. example configures mirrors, checks dependency list, installs missing packages, verifies versions date.
code: library(evanverse)  # 1. Switch to a fast local mirror set_mirror("all", "tuna") #> v CRAN mirror set to: https://mirrors.tuna.tsinghua.edu.cn/CRAN #> v Bioconductor mirror set to: https://mirrors.tuna.tsinghua.edu.cn/bioconductor  # 2. Define required packages cran_pkgs <- c("ggplot2", "dplyr", "purrr", "readxl") bioc_pkgs <- c("DESeq2", "clusterProfiler")  # 3. Check what is already installed cran_status <- check_pkg(cran_pkgs, source = "CRAN") bioc_status <- check_pkg(bioc_pkgs, source = "Bioconductor")  # 4. Install only missing packages missing_cran <- cran_status$name[!cran_status$installed] missing_bioc <- bioc_status$name[!bioc_status$installed]  if (length(missing_cran) > 0) inst_pkg(missing_cran, source = "CRAN") if (length(missing_bioc) > 0) inst_pkg(missing_bioc, source = "Bioconductor")  # 5. Verify versions -flag anything outdated versions <- pkg_version(c(cran_pkgs, bioc_pkgs)) outdated <- versions[!is.na(versions$version) &                      !is.na(versions$latest)   &                      versions$version != versions$latest, ] outdated #>      package version latest source #> 1    ggplot2   3.4.4  3.5.0   CRAN #> 2     DESeq2  1.38.0 1.40.0   Bioconductor  # 6. Update only the outdated ones if (nrow(outdated) > 0) {   cran_out <- outdated$package[outdated$source == "CRAN"]   bioc_out <- outdated$package[outdated$source == "Bioconductor"]   if (length(cran_out) > 0) update_pkg(cran_out, source = "CRAN")   if (length(bioc_out) > 0) update_pkg(bioc_out, source = "Bioconductor") }  # 7. Confirm evanverse exports look right pkg_functions("evanverse", key = "pkg") #> [1] "check_pkg"    "inst_pkg"     "pkg_functions" "pkg_version"  "update_pkg"

title: Plotting Functions
text: typical analysis might use several plot functions together:
code: library(evanverse)  # Group sizes as a sorted bar chart summary_df <- data.frame(   group = c("T_cell", "B_cell", "NK_cell", "Monocyte"),   n     = c(423, 187, 95, 312) )  plot_bar(summary_df, x_col = "group", y_col = "n",          sort = TRUE, horizontal = TRUE) #> # Horizontal sorted bar chart  # Proportional composition as a pie chart plot_pie(summary_df, group_col = "group", count_col = "n", label = "percent") #> # Pie chart with percentage labels  # Score distributions per cell type score_df <- data.frame(   score = c(rnorm(423, 55, 10), rnorm(187, 62, 9),             rnorm(95, 70, 8),   rnorm(312, 48, 12)),   group = rep(c("T_cell", "B_cell", "NK_cell", "Monocyte"),               c(423, 187, 95, 312)) )  plot_density(score_df, x_col = "score", group_col = "group") #> # Overlaid density curves for all four cell types  # DEG overlaps across comparisons deg_tc <- paste0("GENE", sample(1:500, 80)) deg_bc <- paste0("GENE", sample(1:500, 65)) deg_nk <- paste0("GENE", sample(1:500, 45))  plot_venn(deg_tc, deg_bc, deg_nk,           set_names = c("T_cell DEGs", "B_cell DEGs", "NK_cell DEGs")) #> # Three-set Venn of differentially expressed genes

title: Statistical Utilities
text: stat module provides six functions across five common statistical tasks: Note: code examples vignette static (eval = FALSE). Output hand-written reflect current implementation.
code: library(evanverse)

title: Statistical Utilities
text: stat_power() stat_n() support test families:

what: stat_power() - Compute power from sample size
title: Statistical Utilities
text: Given n, effect_size, test settings, returns power_result object computed power plus interpretation recommendation. ANOVA, k required: chi-square, df required: Use S3 methods reporting visualization:
code: res <- stat_power(n = 30, effect_size = 0.5) print(res) #> Power: 47.8%  (very low) | Two-sample t-test #> n = 30 per group,  effect size = 0.500,  alpha = 0.050 stat_power(n = 25, effect_size = 0.25, test = "anova", k = 3) #> # power_result object stat_power(n = 120, effect_size = 0.3, test = "chisq", df = 2) #> # power_result object summary(res) plot(res) #> # A ggplot object: power curve over sample size

title: Statistical Utilities
text: Given target power expected effect_size, returns minimum required sample size (rounded ) power_result object. One-sided correlation example: Use S3 methods stat_power():
code: res_n <- stat_n(power = 0.8, effect_size = 0.5) print(res_n) #> n = 64 per group (128 total) | Two-sample t-test #> Target power = 80%,  effect size = 0.500,  alpha = 0.050 stat_n(   power = 0.9,   effect_size = 0.3,   test = "correlation",   alternative = "greater" ) #> # power_result object summary(res_n) plot(res_n) #> # A ggplot object: required n as a function of effect size

title: Statistical Utilities
text: quick_ttest() compares exactly two groups numeric outcome. method = "auto", chooses t.test wilcox.test based normality checks sample-size-aware rules. Paired comparison requires paired = TRUE id_col:
code: set.seed(42) df <- data.frame(   group = rep(c("A", "B"), each = 30),   value = c(rnorm(30, 5), rnorm(30, 6)) )  res_t <- quick_ttest(df, group_col = "group", value_col = "value") print(res_t) #> t.test | p = 0.0012* | A n=30, B n=30 summary(res_t) set.seed(7) df_p <- data.frame(   id    = rep(1:20, 2),   group = rep(c("pre", "post"), each = 20),   value = c(rnorm(20, 10, 2), rnorm(20, 11, 2)) )  res_p <- quick_ttest(   df_p,   group_col = "group",   value_col = "value",   paired = TRUE,   id_col = "id" ) plot(res_p, plot_type = "both", p_label = "p.format")

title: Statistical Utilities
text: quick_anova() compares 2+ groups numeric outcome performs assumption diagnostics. Auto-selection logic: Check group normality. normality passes, choose ANOVA vs Welch variance equality. normality fails, use Kruskal-Wallis. can force method post-hoc behavior explicitly:
code: set.seed(123) df_a <- data.frame(   group = rep(LETTERS[1:3], each = 40),   value = rnorm(120, mean = rep(c(0, 0.5, 1.2), each = 40), sd = 1) )  res_a <- quick_anova(df_a, group_col = "group", value_col = "value") print(res_a) #> One-way ANOVA | p < 0.001* | A n=40, B n=40, C n=40 summary(res_a) res_a2 <- quick_anova(   df_a,   group_col = "group",   value_col = "value",   method = "anova",   post_hoc = "tukey" ) plot(res_a2, plot_type = "violin")

title: Statistical Utilities
text: quick_chisq() tests association two categorical variables. method = "auto", method choice depends table shape expected frequencies. 2x2 small-sample Fisher example: method = "mcnemar" paired/matched categorical outcomes .
code: set.seed(123) df_c <- data.frame(   treatment = sample(c("A", "B", "C"), 100, replace = TRUE),   response  = sample(c("Success", "Failure"), 100, replace = TRUE,                      prob = c(0.6, 0.4)) )  res_c <- quick_chisq(df_c, x_col = "treatment", y_col = "response") print(res_c) #> Chi-square test | p = 0.0410* | 3x2 | V = 0.20 (small) summary(res_c) df_2x2 <- data.frame(   exposed = c("Yes", "Yes", "No", "No", "Yes", "No"),   event   = c("Yes", "No", "Yes", "No", "Yes", "No") )  res_f <- quick_chisq(df_2x2, x_col = "exposed", y_col = "event", method = "fisher") plot(res_f, plot_type = "heatmap")

what: quick_cor() - Correlation matrix with significance summary
title: Statistical Utilities
text: quick_cor() computes pairwise correlations, p-values, optional multiple-testing adjustment, returns heatmap-ready result object. Restrict variables use BH-adjusted p-values: p_adjust_method != "none", significance based adjusted p-values.
code: res_cor <- quick_cor(mtcars) print(res_cor) #> pearson | 11 vars | 24/55 significant pairs (alpha = 0.05) summary(res_cor) res_cor2 <- quick_cor(   mtcars,   vars = c("mpg", "hp", "wt", "qsec"),   method = "spearman",   p_adjust_method = "BH" ) plot(res_cor2, type = "upper", show_sig = TRUE)

title: Statistical Utilities
text: planning analysis functions compose naturally practice:
code: library(evanverse)  # 1. Plan sample size plan <- stat_n(power = 0.8, effect_size = 0.5, test = "t_two")  # 2. Collect / prepare data and run a primary two-group test set.seed(101) df <- data.frame(   group = rep(c("Control", "Treatment"), each = plan$n),   value = c(rnorm(plan$n, 10, 2), rnorm(plan$n, 11, 2)) ) res_t <- quick_ttest(df, group_col = "group", value_col = "value")  # 3. Explore correlation structure among related numeric variables res_c <- quick_cor(mtcars[, c("mpg", "disp", "hp", "wt", "qsec")])  # 4. Report + visualize print(res_t) plot(res_t) print(res_c) plot(res_c, type = "upper", show_sig = TRUE)

what: toy_gene_ref() - Generate a compact gene reference table
title: Toy Data Utilities
text: toy_gene_ref() returns small reference table compatible gene2entrez() / gene2ensembl() workflows. Key behavior: species supports "human" "mouse". n controls returned rows capped 100 available rows. Missing symbols returned NA (never empty strings). Mouse example: n larger 100 silently capped: Invalid n (0, negative, non-integer) raises error.
code: ref_human <- toy_gene_ref() head(ref_human, 3) #>          symbol       ensembl_id entrez_id      gene_type species ensembl_version download_date #> 1        RNA5S5 ENSG00000199396 124905431           rRNA   human             113    2025-04-24 #> 2          <NA> ENSG00000295528        NA         lncRNA   human             113    2025-04-24 #> 3          <NA> ENSG00000301748        NA         lncRNA   human             113    2025-04-24 ref_mouse <- toy_gene_ref(species = "mouse", n = 10) ref_mouse[, c("symbol", "ensembl_id", "species")] #>          symbol          ensembl_id species #> 1          <NA> ENSMUSG00000123309   mouse #> 2       Gm45096 ENSMUSG00000108739   mouse #> ... nrow(toy_gene_ref(n = 999)) #> [1] 100

what: toy_gmt() - Write a temporary GMT file
title: Toy Data Utilities
text: toy_gmt() writes GMT file temporary path returns file path. designed feed directly gmt2df() gmt2list(). Key behavior: Default n = 5 writes 5 gene sets. n capped 5 available built-sets. Every line GMT-formatted: term, description, genes. Invalid n (0, negative, non-integer) raises error.
code: path <- toy_gmt() path #> [1] "C:/Users/.../Rtmp.../file....gmt"  readLines(path)[1] #> [1] "HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY\tGenes regulated by p53\tTP53\tBRCA1\tMYC\tEGFR\t..." length(readLines(toy_gmt(n = 1))) #> [1] 1  length(readLines(toy_gmt(n = 3))) #> [1] 3  length(readLines(toy_gmt(n = 99))) #> [1] 5

title: Toy Data Utilities
code: tmp <- toy_gmt(n = 3)  df <- gmt2df(tmp) head(df, 4) #>                    term               description  gene #> 1 HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY   Genes regulated by p53  TP53 #> 2 HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY   Genes regulated by p53 BRCA1 #> 3 HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY   Genes regulated by p53   MYC #> 4 HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY   Genes regulated by p53  EGFR  lst <- gmt2list(tmp) names(lst) #> [1] "HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY" "HALLMARK_MTORC1_SIGNALING" "HALLMARK_HYPOXIA"

title: Toy Data Utilities
text: common offline testing flow : Build small reference toy_gene_ref(). Build small gene-set file toy_gmt(). Parse GMT convert symbols IDs using base converters.
code: library(evanverse)  # 1. Toy reference ref <- toy_gene_ref(species = "human", n = 100)  # 2. Toy gene sets path <- toy_gmt(n = 3) long <- gmt2df(path)  # 3. Convert symbols to Entrez IDs id_map <- gene2entrez(long$gene, ref = ref, species = "human") long$entrez_id <- id_map$entrez_id  # 4. Rebuild list of Entrez IDs per term long2 <- long[!is.na(long$entrez_id), ] sets_entrez <- df2list(long2, group_col = "term", value_col = "entrez_id")  names(sets_entrez) #> [1] "HALLMARK_P53_PATHWAY" "HALLMARK_MTORC1_SIGNALING" "HALLMARK_HYPOXIA"

what: Case-insensitive mapping returning a named vector — %map%
title: Case-insensitive mapping returning a named vector — %map%
text: Like %match%, returns named character vector instead indices. Names canonical entries table; values original elements x. Unmatched values dropped.

title: Case-insensitive mapping returning a named vector — %map%
code: x %map% table

title: Case-insensitive mapping returning a named vector — %map%
text: x Character vector input strings. table Character vector match .

title: Case-insensitive mapping returning a named vector — %map%
text: named character vector. Names canonical entries table; values original elements x. Order follows x (table). Unmatched entries dropped.

title: Case-insensitive mapping returning a named vector — %map%
code: c("tp53", "brca1", "egfr") %map% c("TP53", "EGFR", "MYC") #>   TP53   EGFR  #> "tp53" "egfr"  # returns: TP53 = "tp53", EGFR = "egfr"

title: Case-insensitive match returning indices — %match%
text: Like base::match(), ignores letter case. Useful gene ID matching.

title: Case-insensitive match returning indices — %match%
code: x %match% table

title: Case-insensitive match returning indices — %match%
text: x Character vector match. table Character vector values match .

title: Case-insensitive match returning indices — %match%
text: integer vector match positions. Returns NA non-matches.

title: Case-insensitive match returning indices — %match%
text: x table must non-empty character vectors; character(0) non-character inputs raise error. differs base::match() %nin%, accept empty vectors. stricter contract intentional gene-ID workflows empty query almost always signals upstream mistake.

title: Case-insensitive match returning indices — %match%
code: c("tp53", "BRCA1", "egfr") %match% c("TP53", "EGFR", "MYC") #> [1]  1 NA  2 # returns: 1 NA 2

title: Not-in operator — %nin%
text: Tests whether elements x present table. Equivalent !(x %% table). NA behaviour follows base R semantics.

title: Not-in operator — %nin%
code: x %nin% table

title: Not-in operator — %nin%
text: x vector values test. table vector values test .

title: Not-in operator — %nin%
text: logical vector.

title: Not-in operator — %nin%
code: c("A", "B", "C") %nin% c("B", "D")   # TRUE FALSE TRUE #> [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE 1:5 %nin% c(2, 4)                    # TRUE FALSE TRUE FALSE TRUE #> [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE

title: Publication-ready forest plot — plot_forest
text: Produces publication-ready forest plot UKB-standard styling. user supplies data frame whose first column row label (item), plus additional display columns (e.g. Cases/N). gap column auto-formatted (95% CI) text column inserted automatically ci_column. Numeric p-value columns declared via p_cols formatted -place.

title: Publication-ready forest plot — plot_forest
code: plot_forest(   data,   est,   lower,   upper,   ci_column = 2L,   ref_line = 1,   xlim = NULL,   ticks_at = NULL,   arrow_lab = c("Lower risk", "Higher risk"),   header = NULL,   indent = NULL,   bold_label = NULL,   ci_col = "black",   ci_sizes = 0.6,   ci_Theight = 0.2,   ci_digits = 2L,   ci_sep = ", ",   p_cols = NULL,   p_digits = 3L,   bold_p = TRUE,   p_threshold = 0.05,   align = NULL,   background = "zebra",   bg_col = "#F0F0F0",   border = "three_line",   border_width = 3,   row_height = NULL,   col_width = NULL,   save = FALSE,   dest = NULL,   save_width = 20,   save_height = NULL,   theme = "default" )

title: Publication-ready forest plot — plot_forest
text: data data.frame. First column must label column (item). Additional columns displayed -(character) formatted named p_cols (must numeric). Column order preserved. est Numeric vector. Point estimates (NA = CI drawn). lower Numeric vector. Lower CI bounds (length est). upper Numeric vector. Upper CI bounds (length est). ci_column Integer. Column position final rendered table gap/CI graphic placed. Must 2 ncol(data) + 1 (inclusive). Default: 2L. ref_line Numeric. Reference line. Default: 1 (HR/). Use 0 beta coefficients. xlim Numeric vector length 2. X-axis limits. NULL (default) uses c(0, 2). ticks_at Numeric vector. Tick positions. NULL (default) = 5 evenly spaced ticks across xlim. arrow_lab Character vector length 2. Directional labels. Default: c("Lower risk", "Higher risk"). NULL = none. header Character vector length ncol(data) + 2. Column header labels final rendered table (original columns + gap_ci + label). NULL (default) = use column names data plus "gap_ci" "(95% CI)". Pass "" gap column position. indent Integer vector (length = nrow(data)). Indentation level label column: unit adds two leading spaces. Default: zeros. bold_label Logical vector (length = nrow(data)). rows bold label column. NULL (default) = auto-derive indent: rows indent == 0 bolded (parent rows), indented sub-rows plain. ci_col Character scalar vector (length = nrow(data)). CI colour(s). NA rows skipped automatically. Default: "black". ci_sizes Numeric. Point size. Default: 0.6. ci_Theight Numeric. CI cap height. Default: 0.2. ci_digits Integer. Decimal places auto-generated (95% CI) column. Default: 2L. ci_sep Character. Separator lower upper CI label, e.g. ", " " - ". Default: ", ". p_cols Character vector. Names numeric p-value columns data. formatted p_digits decimal places "<0.001"-style clipping. NULL = none. p_digits Integer. Decimal places p-value formatting. Default: 3L. bold_p TRUE (bold non-NA p p_threshold), FALSE (bolding), logical vector (per-row control). Default: TRUE. p_threshold Numeric. P-value threshold bolding bold_p = TRUE. Default: 0.05. align Integer vector length ncol(data) + 2. Alignment per column: -1 left, 0 centre, 1 right. Must cover final columns (original + gap_ci + label). NULL = auto (column 1 left, others centre). background Character. Row background style: "zebra", "bold_label" (shade rows bold_label = TRUE), "none". Default: "zebra". bg_col Character. Shading colour backgrounds (scalar), per-row vector length nrow(data) (overrides style). Default: "#F0F0F0". border Character. Border style: "three_line" "none". Default: "three_line". border_width Numeric. Border line width(s). Scalar = three lines width; length-3 vector = top--header, bottom--header, bottom--body. Default: 3. row_height NULL (auto), numeric scalar, numeric vector (length = total gtable rows including margins). Auto sets 8 / 12 / 10 / 15 mm top / header / data / bottom respectively. col_width NULL (auto), numeric scalar, numeric vector (length = total gtable columns). Auto rounds default width adjustment \([5, 10)\) mm. save Logical. Save output files? Default: FALSE. dest Character. Destination file path (extension ignored; four formats saved). Required save = TRUE. save_width Numeric. Output width cm. Default: 20. save_height Numeric NULL. Output height cm. NULL = nrow(data) * 0.9 + 3. theme Character preset ("default") forestploter::forest_theme object. Default: "default".

title: Publication-ready forest plot — plot_forest

title: One-way comparison with automatic method selection — quick_anova
text: Auto method selection logic: Normality checked per group via Shapiro-Wilk (sample-size-adaptive thresholds — see quick_ttest details). normality passes: Levene's test decides classical ANOVA (equal variances) Welch ANOVA (unequal variances). normality fails: Kruskal-Wallis used. Post-hoc defaults post_hoc = "auto": "anova" → Tukey HSD "welch" → Pairwise Welch t-tests (BH-adjusted) "kruskal" → Pairwise Wilcoxon (BH-adjusted)

title: One-way comparison with automatic method selection — quick_anova
code: set.seed(123) df <- data.frame(   group = rep(LETTERS[1:3], each = 40),   value = rnorm(120, mean = rep(c(0, 0.5, 1.2), each = 40), sd = 1) ) result <- quick_anova(df, group_col = "group", value_col = "value") print(result) #> One-way ANOVA | p < 0.001* | A n=40, B n=40, C n=40 summary(result) #>  #> ── One-way Comparison ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Parameters ── #>  #> Test: One-way ANOVA #> alpha: 0.050 #>  #> ── Omnibus Test ── #>  #> ✔ p < 0.001  (significant at alpha = 0.05) #> eta_squared = 0.224, omega_squared = 0.210 #>  #>              Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value   Pr(>F)     #> group         2  27.51  13.755   16.91 3.53e-07 *** #> Residuals   117  95.14   0.813                      #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 #>  #> ── Descriptive statistics ── #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 7 #>   group     n   mean    sd median    min   max #>   <fct> <int>  <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 A        40 0.0452 0.898 0.0906 -1.97   1.79 #> 2 B        40 0.493  0.960 0.437  -1.81   2.67 #> 3 C        40 1.21   0.844 1.15   -0.468  3.39 #> ── Normality (Shapiro-Wilk) ── #>  #> A: n = 40, p = 0.953 #> B: n = 40, p = 0.940 #> C: n = 40, p = 0.913 #> -> Medium samples (min n = 40). Data reasonably normal (all Shapiro p ≥ 0.01). #>  #> ── Variance (Levene's test) ── #>  #> p = 0.854 | equal variances: TRUE #>  #> ── Post-hoc (tukey) ── #>  #> # A tibble: 3 × 6 #>   group2 group1  diff     lwr   upr     `p adj` #>   <chr>  <chr>  <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl>       <dbl> #> 1 B      A      0.448 -0.0306 0.927 0.0716      #> 2 C      A      1.16   0.684  1.64  0.000000201 #> 3 C      B      0.715  0.236  1.19  0.00163     #>  plot(result)

title: Categorical association test with automatic method selection — quick_chisq
text: Auto method selection logic: 2×2 table expected frequency < 5: Fisher's exact test >20\ 2x2 table 5 <= expected frequency < 10: Yates' correction applied Otherwise: standard chi-square test WARNING: "mcnemar" paired/matched data (e.g., -measurements subjects). use independent samples — use "chisq" "fisher" instead.

title: Categorical association test with automatic method selection — quick_chisq
code: set.seed(123) df <- data.frame(   treatment = sample(c("A", "B", "C"), 100, replace = TRUE),   response  = sample(c("Success", "Failure"), 100, replace = TRUE,                      prob = c(0.6, 0.4)) ) result <- quick_chisq(df, x_col = "treatment", y_col = "response") print(result) #> Chi-square test | p = 0.9568 | 3x2 | V = 0.03 (negligible) summary(result) #>  #> ── Categorical Association Test ──────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Parameters ── #>  #> Test: Chi-square test #> Variables: treatment × response #> Table size: 3x2 #> alpha: 0.050 #>  #> ── Result ── #>  #> ℹ p = 0.9568  (not significant at alpha = 0.05) #>  #>  #> 	Pearson's Chi-squared test #>  #> data:  cont_table #> X-squared = 0.088413, df = 2, p-value = 0.9568 #>  #>  #> ── Effect Size (Cramer's V) ── #>  #> V: 0.03 #> Interpretation: negligible #>  #> ── Observed Frequencies ── #>  #>     #>     Failure Success #>   A      13      20 #>   B      13      19 #>   C      13      22 #> ── Expected Frequencies ── #>  #>   Failure Success #> A   12.87   20.13 #> B   12.48   19.52 #> C   13.65   21.35 #> ── Pearson Residuals ── #>  #>     #>     Failure Success #>   A    0.04   -0.03 #>   B    0.15   -0.12 #>   C   -0.18    0.14 #> → |residual| > 2 indicates significant deviation from independence #>  #> ── Method Selection ── #>  #> Table size: 3x2 #> Total N: 100 #> Min expected freq: 12.48 #> Cells with freq < 5: 0 #> Decision: All expected frequencies adequate: using standard chi-square test #>  plot(result)

title: Calculate Required Sample Size — stat_n
text: power Numeric. Target statistical power. Default: 0.8. effect_size Numeric. Effect size appropriate chosen test (must positive). title: evanverse 0.3.0
text: focused refactor release: unified CLI messaging, CRAN-safe startup, tighter parameter validation, stronger tests, several new utilities.

title: evanverse 0.3.0
text: plot_forest() — Forest plots publication-style defaults (forestploter), significance bolding, color recycling, alignment options. plot_bar() — Bar charts optional fill grouping, sorting, vertical/horizontal layout, clean defaults. read_excel_flex() — Enhanced Excel reader (readxl) optional name cleaning (janitor), range/col_types controls, CLI feedback. write_xlsx_flex() — Flexible Excel writer (openxlsx) header styling, auto column width, overwrite/timestamp options. view() — Quick interactive data viewer (reactable) exploration/QC. set_mirror() — Switch CRAN/Bioconductor mirrors predefined, named endpoints. pkg_functions() — List exported functions installed package, optional keyword filtering.

title: evanverse 0.3.0
text: Inlined, robust delimiter detection .csv/.tsv/.txt .gz files Stricter parameter checks clearer CLI error messages Enhanced encoding guardrails better file handling Source normalization via strict match() function Supports “CRAN”, “GitHub”, “Bioconductor” sources Clearer messages mirror handling aligned set_mirror() robust CRAN/Bioconductor database retrieval Helpful suggestions GitHub package installations Optional preview printing clearer CLI output %% %near% messages standardized (emoji) Tighter type/shape checks cleaner examples %nin% implemented Negate(%%) simple, predictable NA semantics map_column() uses unified cli output counts unmatched keys Skips numeric columns design, removes emoji, adds modular separators with_timer() simplified using tictoc timing cli output remind() rendering fixed cli braces partial/case-insensitive matches rgb2hex() input validation tightened concise CLI feedback rows_with_void() header streamlined, delegates detection is_void() .onAttach switched plain cli::cli_text() messages (emoji), CRAN-safe Roxygen examples trimmed non-interactive/CRAN environments Consistent headers separators throughout documentation

title: evanverse 0.3.0
text: Tests modularized expanded per-test skip_on_cran() / skip_if_not_installed() Removed nested test_that() blocks better organization Strengthened assertions tolerant regex messages avoid brittle failures Added/updated coverage plotting functions: plot_forest(), plot_bar() File /O functions: read_table_flex(), read_excel_flex(), write_xlsx_flex() Package management: set_mirror(), pkg_functions() Utilities: remind(), map_column(), replace_void(), rows_with_void(), rgb2hex() Operators: %%, %near%, %nin%, %p% Marked environment-dependent tests (e.g., file_info()) skip_on_cran() Ensured tests work reliably across different platforms CI environments
